title: Price elasticity of supply
summary: Price elasticity of supply (PES) measures how responsive producers are to a change in the price of their good. This Higher Level lesson builds the formula, the full spectrum of PES values from perfectly inelastic to perfectly elastic, the determinants that decide where a good sits on that spectrum — above all the time period — and why primary commodities tend to have low PES while manufactures have higher PES. Because this is HL content assessed on Paper 3, every idea is tied to worked numerical examples and to how our marking engine awards the quantitative marks.
learningObjectives: Define, calculate and interpret price elasticity of supply (PES) using PES = %ΔQs / %ΔP.
Distinguish and illustrate the five PES cases: perfectly inelastic, inelastic, unit elastic, elastic and perfectly elastic.
Explain the determinants of PES — time period, spare capacity, ability to store stocks, mobility of factors and ease of switching production.
Explain why primary commodities tend to have low PES while manufactured goods tend to have higher PES.
Answer Paper 3 quantitative questions on PES with full working, showing the method marks examiners look for.
levelNote: HL only — Higher Level content assessed on Paper 3.

simpleExplanation.title: How Quickly Can Producers React to a Price Change?
simpleExplanation.summary: Price elasticity of supply (PES) measures how much the quantity firms are willing and able to supply changes when the price changes. A high PES means producers can ramp output up or down quickly; a low PES means they are stuck, at least for now.
simpleExplanation.analogy: Think about two sellers on the day the price of their product jumps. A T-shirt printer with idle machines and blank stock in the back room can print and ship thousands more by tomorrow — highly responsive, high PES. An oak-furniture maker whose timber needs decades to grow and whose workshop is already full cannot make more this year no matter how tempting the price — very unresponsive, low PES. Same price rise, completely different ability to respond.
steps: Find the percentage change in price. Say the price of steel rises from $500 to $550 per tonne — a 10% rise.
Find the resulting percentage change in quantity supplied. Mills raise output by 5%.
Interpret it. Because PES is below 1, supply is price inelastic — producers cannot expand output as fast as the price rose, since new capacity takes time to bring online.

flashcards.back: A measure of the responsiveness of the quantity supplied of a good to a change in its own price. It captures how willing and able producers are to change output.
PES = (% change in quantity supplied) ÷ (% change in price) = %ΔQs / %ΔP. Each percentage change is measured relative to the ORIGINAL value.
Because of the law of supply: price and quantity supplied move in the SAME direction. So the ratio is always zero or positive — never negative, unlike PED.
Quantity supplied changes by a LARGER percentage than price. Producers are very responsive. The supply curve is relatively flat and, if extended, cuts the price (vertical) axis.
Quantity supplied changes by a SMALLER percentage than price. Producers are unresponsive. The supply curve is relatively steep and, if extended, cuts the quantity (horizontal) axis.
Quantity supplied changes by exactly the same percentage as price. Any straight-line supply curve drawn through the ORIGIN is unit elastic, whatever its slope.
Quantity supplied is fixed and does not change at all when price changes — a vertical supply curve. Example: seats in a fixed stadium on match day.
Producers will supply any quantity at one particular price but nothing below it — a horizontal supply curve. A useful limiting case rather than a common real market.

The single most important determinant. Momentary/market period: often perfectly inelastic. Short run: some factors variable, so more elastic. Long run: all factors variable, so supply is most elastic.
Idle machines and underemployed workers let a firm raise output quickly and cheaply when price rises, so spare capacity raises PES (makes supply more elastic).

flashcards.front: Ability to store stocks and PES
flashcards.back: Firms that can hold inventories can release stock the moment price rises, so storable goods have higher PES. Perishable goods and services (which cannot be stored) have lower PES.

Primary commodities (crops, minerals) tend to have LOW PES because of long production/biological time lags and specialised, immobile factors. Manufactures tend to have HIGHER PES because output can be scaled up faster with mobile, general-purpose resources.
Always divide the change by the ORIGINAL (starting) value, not the new value or the average. Using the wrong base is the most common way students lose accuracy marks on PES calculations.

quickCheck.prompt: The correct formula for price elasticity of supply is:
For a given good, supply is generally MOST elastic in the:
A firm with large amounts of unused (spare) capacity will, other things equal, have supply that is:
options: More elastic, because output can be raised quickly
Less elastic, because it is already producing
quickCheck.answer: More elastic, because output can be raised quickly

quickCheck.prompt: Compared with manufactured goods, primary commodities such as coffee or copper typically have:
options: Higher PES, because factors are highly mobile
Lower PES, because of long production lags and specialised factors
quickCheck.answer: Lower PES, because of long production lags and specialised factors

quickCheck.prompt: When price rises from $20 to $25 and quantity supplied rises from 400 to 460 units, PES equals:

faq.q: Why is PES always positive while PED is usually negative?
faq.a: It comes down to the underlying law. The law of supply says price and quantity supplied move in the same direction — a higher price makes production more profitable, so firms offer more. Because both the top and bottom of the PES fraction move the same way, the result is non-negative. PED is usually negative because the law of demand has price and quantity demanded moving in OPPOSITE directions.

faq.q: Can the PES of one product really change over time?
faq.a: Yes, and this is central to the topic. The same good can be perfectly inelastic in the momentary period, mildly inelastic in the short run, and elastic in the long run. Housing is the classic case: after a price surge, the existing stock cannot grow overnight, but over several years builders can add supply, so PES rises with the time horizon.

faq.q: Why do primary commodities tend to have low PES?
faq.a: Primary production is hard to speed up. Crops depend on a growing season, mines and oil wells take years and heavy investment to expand, and the specialised land, skills and equipment involved cannot easily be switched in from other uses. So when the price of a commodity rises, quantity supplied responds only weakly in the short term — low PES. Manufactured goods, by contrast, can often be scaled up quickly using general-purpose machinery and mobile labour, giving higher PES.

faq.q: How is PES useful when analysing a tax or subsidy on Paper 3?
faq.a: PES helps predict how a per-unit tax or subsidy is shared and how large the quantity effect is. When supply is inelastic, producers absorb more of a tax and quantity changes little; when supply is elastic, the quantity effect is larger and more of the burden can be passed to consumers. Knowing PES therefore lets you reason about tax incidence, revenue and welfare — all of which appear in HL Paper 3 questions.

sections.content: In the previous elasticity topics we asked how buyers respond to price. Now we turn the model around and ask about sellers. When the price of a good rises, can producers pour more onto the market, or are they stuck with what they already make? Why can a software firm satisfy a surge in demand almost instantly while a coffee grower can do almost nothing until the next harvest? Price elasticity of supply (PES) answers exactly this, and because it is Higher Level content examined on Paper 3, we will keep everything anchored to numbers and to the marks a calculation actually earns.

Price elasticity of supply measures the responsiveness of the quantity supplied of a good to a change in its own price. A large PES means firms can change output easily and quickly; a small PES means output is hard to change, at least in the time available. Because the responsiveness is expressed as a ratio of percentage changes, PES is a pure number with no units, which lets us compare very different goods on the same scale.
PES = \frac{\% \text{ change in quantity supplied}}{\% \text{ change in price}} = \frac{\%\Delta Q_s}{\%\Delta P}

Each percentage change is measured against the ORIGINAL value: %ΔP = (new price − old price) ÷ old price × 100, and likewise for quantity. Getting that base right is what separates a correct answer from a near miss in an exam.

items: PES is the ratio of the percentage change in quantity supplied to the percentage change in price.
PES is always zero or positive, because price and quantity supplied move in the same direction (the law of supply).
A larger PES means greater responsiveness — producers can adjust output more freely.
Percentage changes are always taken relative to the starting (original) value, not the new value or the midpoint.

sections.question: The market price of copper rises by 12% over a year. In response, global mines increase the quantity supplied by 3%. Calculate the PES for copper and interpret your answer.
sections.solution:
Step 1 — Apply the formula.
PES = %ΔQs ÷ %ΔP = 3% ÷ 12%.

Step 2 — Compute.
PES = 0.25.

Interpretation: because PES lies between 0 and 1, the supply of copper is price INELASTIC. Quantity supplied rose by far less (3%) than price (12%), because opening or expanding mines takes years of investment — a textbook reason primary commodities respond weakly to price in the short run.

sections.content: The PES number tells a story, and it is worth learning the five cases and how each looks as a supply curve. The two extreme cases — perfectly inelastic and perfectly elastic — are limiting benchmarks that make the middle three easier to picture.

items: **Perfectly inelastic (PES = 0):** quantity supplied is completely fixed; the supply curve is vertical. Example: seats in a fixed stadium on the night, or a Old Master painting.
**Inelastic (0 < PES < 1):** quantity supplied changes proportionally less than price; a relatively steep curve that, extended, cuts the quantity (horizontal) axis.
**Unit elastic (PES = 1):** quantity supplied changes in exactly the same proportion as price; ANY straight-line supply curve drawn through the origin, whatever its slope.
**Elastic (1 < PES < ∞):** quantity supplied changes proportionally more than price; a relatively flat curve that, extended, cuts the price (vertical) axis.
**Perfectly elastic (PES = ∞):** producers supply any amount at one price and nothing below it; a horizontal supply curve.

sections.content: For diagram marks, remember the intercept rule. A straight-line supply curve through the ORIGIN is unit elastic wherever it sits. If it is extended and cuts the QUANTITY axis it is inelastic; if it cuts the PRICE axis it is elastic. Labelling that intercept is a fast, reliable way to prove your curve shows the elasticity you claim.
The elasticity of supply is not arbitrary — it follows from how production works. For Paper 3 you need to both state a determinant AND explain the direction in which it pushes PES.

items: **Time period — the most important determinant.** In the momentary (market) period, supply is often perfectly inelastic: nothing extra can be produced right now. In the short run at least one factor is fixed, but firms can work existing capacity harder, so supply becomes somewhat elastic. In the long run every factor is variable — new plant can be built, new workers trained — so supply is most elastic of all.

**Spare (unused) capacity.** Idle machinery and underemployed labour let a firm lift output quickly and cheaply when price rises, raising PES. A firm already at full capacity has little room to respond, lowering PES.

**Ability to store stocks.** If a good can be stockpiled cheaply (canned food, electronics, raw metals), firms can release inventory the instant price rises, so PES is higher. Perishable goods and services — which cannot be stored — have lower PES.

**Mobility of factors of production.** If land, labour and capital can be shifted easily between products, firms can redirect resources toward the good whose price has risen, raising PES. Highly specialised, immobile factors lower PES.

**Ease of switching production.** Related to mobility: if a producer can readily switch its production lines from one good to another (a farmer moving between similar crops, a factory retooling quickly), supply is more elastic. Where switching is slow or costly, PES is low.

sections.content: Why primary commodities have low PES and manufactures higher PES

Bringing the determinants together explains a pattern examiners love to test. Primary commodities — agricultural crops, minerals, oil — tend to have LOW PES. Farming is bound by biological time lags: a crop cannot be grown faster than a season, and once harvested the quantity available is fixed until the next one. Mines, wells and plantations take years and large sunk investments to expand, and the specialised land and skills involved cannot easily be drawn in from elsewhere. So a price rise brings only a weak short-run supply response.

Manufactured goods tend to have HIGHER PES. Factories can add shifts, use spare capacity, hold and release inventories, and rely on general-purpose machinery and mobile labour that can be scaled up or switched between products comparatively quickly. The result is that when the price of a manufactured good rises, quantity supplied can respond more fully and faster. This contrast — inelastic primary supply versus more elastic manufactured supply — underlies much of the analysis of commodity price volatility later in the course.

items: Primary commodities: long/biological production lags, specialised and immobile factors, limited short-run capacity → LOW PES.
Manufactures: spare capacity, storable output, mobile general-purpose factors, easier switching → HIGHER PES.
Because inelastic supply cannot cushion demand shifts, low-PES commodity markets show larger price swings — a key link to price volatility.

sections.question: A furniture manufacturer sells tables at $200 each and supplies 8,000 tables a year while running its factory well below capacity. Demand rises and the price climbs to $230; using its idle capacity the firm raises output to 9,600 tables a year. Calculate the PES and comment on the result.
sections.solution:
Step 1 — Percentage change in price.
%ΔP = (230 − 200) ÷ 200 × 100 = 30 ÷ 200 × 100 = 15%.

Step 2 — Percentage change in quantity supplied.
%ΔQs = (9,600 − 8,000) ÷ 8,000 × 100 = 1,600 ÷ 8,000 × 100 = 20%.

Step 3 — Apply the formula.
PES = %ΔQs ÷ %ΔP = 20% ÷ 15% = 1.33.

Comment: PES = 1.33 (> 1), so supply is price ELASTIC. Quantity supplied rose proportionally more (20%) than price (15%). The reason is stated in the question — the firm had SPARE CAPACITY, a determinant that makes supply more responsive because output can be raised quickly and at low extra cost.

sections.content: Paper 3 model answer — mapped to our marking engine

sections.question: Paper 3 quantitative: When price rises from $10 to $13, quantity supplied rises from 500 to 620 units. Calculate the PES and state whether supply is elastic or inelastic. [3]
sections.solution:
**Model answer:**
Step 1 — Percentage change in quantity supplied.
%ΔQs = (620 − 500) ÷ 500 × 100 = 120 ÷ 500 × 100 = 24%.

Step 2 — Percentage change in price.
%ΔP = (13 − 10) ÷ 10 × 100 = 3 ÷ 10 × 100 = 30%.

Step 3 — Apply the PES formula.
PES = %ΔQs ÷ %ΔP = 24% ÷ 30% = 0.8.

Step 4 — Interpret.
PES = 0.8, which is between 0 and 1, so supply is price INELASTIC.

---
**How our marking engine awards the marks [3]:**
Paragraph 3 questions on Paper 3 are marked on POINTS, rewarding correct method (M), accuracy (A) and working shown, with follow-through carried between steps.
- **M1 — method for the percentage changes:** shows %ΔQs = 120/500 and %ΔP = 3/10 computed from the ORIGINAL values (24% and 30%). The method mark is for the correct approach even before the final number.
- **M1 — method for PES:** divides %ΔQs by %ΔP the right way up (Qs on top, P on the bottom) — a candidate who inverts the fraction loses this method mark.
- **A1 — accuracy and interpretation:** the correct final value 0.8 AND the correct judgement that supply is inelastic. Working must be visible: a bare '0.8' without the percentage steps cannot earn the method marks.
- **Follow-through:** if a candidate makes an arithmetic slip in an early percentage but then divides correctly and classifies their own value consistently (e.g. calls a value below 1 'inelastic'), the later marks are still awarded on their figure. This is why writing every step out protects your score — the engine can only follow through on working it can see.

sections.content: This is exactly what the Practice button does. Hit it, write your own full solution to a question like this one, and our marking engine grades it on the same POINTS scheme — awarding the method marks for your percentage changes and correctly-oriented formula, the accuracy mark for the value and the elastic/inelastic judgement, and applying follow-through to your own figures. The habit that earns full marks on Paper 3: show every line of working, never just the final number.

items: **Inverting the formula.** PES is %ΔQs ÷ %ΔP, with quantity on top. Writing %ΔP ÷ %ΔQs gives the reciprocal and loses the method mark.
**Using the wrong percentage base.** Divide each change by the ORIGINAL value, not the new value or the average — the most common source of a wrong PES.
**Making PES negative.** Unlike PED, PES is non-negative because price and quantity supplied move together. A negative PES signals an error.

**Ignoring the time period.** Calling a good's supply simply 'inelastic' misses marks — supply that is inelastic in the momentary period may be elastic in the long run. Always tie PES to a time horizon.
**Assuming spare capacity makes supply LESS elastic.** It is the opposite: spare capacity lets output rise quickly, so it RAISES PES.

**Claiming primary and manufactured goods have the same PES.** Primary commodities tend to be inelastic (production lags, immobile factors); manufactures tend to be more elastic. State the reason, not just the result.
**Writing only the final number.** On Paper 3, a bare answer with no working forfeits the method (M) marks and blocks follow-through — always show every step.

sections.content: PES is not a stand-alone calculation — it drives real analysis. Because primary-commodity supply is inelastic, small shifts in demand cause large price swings, which motivates buffer-stock schemes and price-support policies. On Paper 3 you will combine PES with taxes, subsidies and price controls to work out who bears a burden and how quantity responds. Master the calculation and the determinants here, and those later questions become an application of one idea you already own.

sections.label: Get a Paper 3 PES calculation marked: show your working and see the M/A points awarded
